title: Temporal State Tomography via Quantum Snapshotting the Temporal Quasiprobabilities
abstract: Quantum tomography is a cornerstone of quantum information science, enabling the reconstruction of states and channels from experimental data. Here we introduce a new paradigm, temporal state tomography (TST), for reconstructing quantum processes across multiple times. Our approach is based on temporal quasiprobability distributions (TQDs), which, in the informationally complete setting, provide a complete description of multi-time quantum processes and uniquely determine temporal states. We formulate TST as a unified framework for reconstructing both density operators and quantum channels within a single scheme. We show that any TQD can be obtained via classical post-processing of measurement outcomes generated by a fixed set of quantum instruments, thereby establishing a direct operational route to accessing TQDs experimentally. For informationally complete TQDs, the associated temporal state can be reconstructed via a temporal Bloch-type representation. Leveraging this correspondence, we derive the sample complexity of TST, thereby quantifying its statistical efficiency.
summary: The paper introduces Temporal State Tomography, a new method to reconstruct quantum processes across multiple time steps using temporal quasiprobability distributions. It demonstrates that complex temporal dynamics can be recovered through simple sequential measurements and provides a theoretical guarantee on the number of experimental samples needed for accurate reconstruction.
main_problem: Developing a unified framework for Temporal State Tomography (TST) to reconstruct multi-time quantum processes, including both density operators and quantum channels, from experimental data.
main_result: The paper establishes that any temporal quasiprobability distribution (TQD) can be accessed via classical post-processing of quantum instruments and derives the sample complexity required for faithful reconstruction.
method: The authors use 'quantum snapshotting' to transform TQDs into physically realizable sequential measurements and employ the Choi-Jamiołkowski isomorphism and Hoeffding inequalities to bound reconstruction error.
model_or_system: The model considers a multi-time quantum process characterized by an initial state and a sequence of time-ordered, completely positive trace-preserving (CPTP) maps across multiple time instances.
key_observables: Temporal quasiprobability distributions (TQDs), specifically Kirkwood-Dirac and Margenau-Hill distributions, and temporal Pauli correlators.
important_parameters: Sample complexity (N), error tolerance (epsilon), failure probability (delta), and the dimension of the temporal state space (d).
main_assumptions: The reconstruction assumes the use of informationally complete (IC) instruments and that the temporal state admits a specific linear expansion.
figures_summary: Figure 1(a) illustrates the generalization of multipartite density operators to include timelike correlations, while Figure 1(b) shows the construction of a temporal phase space for quantum trajectories.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of temporal states, defines the TQD-based reconstruction framework, proposes the quantum snapshotting protocol, and concludes with a formal mathematical proof of the sample complexity and error bounds.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides a rigorous method for characterizing the dynamics of time-dependent quantum processes and temporal correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Temporal quantum states generalize the multipar- tite density operator formalism to the time domain, enabling a unified description of quantum systems with both timelike and spacelike correlations.(b) By selecting an appropriate phase space at each time step, one can construct a temporal phase space; each quantum trajectory within this temporal phase space is associated with a corresponding temporal quasiproba- bility distribution that encodes temporal quantum correlations.
